body_txt: Animated Shock Comedy is a subgenre of primarily Western Animated Sitcom that emerged in the 1990s , towards the tail end of The Renaissance Age of Animation (though it has roots significantly earlier in the short wave of adult animated comedies sparked by Ralph Bakshi in The '70s ). A new generation of cartoons busted Western TV animation out of the Animation Age Ghetto by infusing animation with pervasive elements that are decidedly not for children: Vulgar Humor and Black Comedy . These shows typically feature heavy elements of Comedic Sociopathy , strong profanity, pop culture references, socio-political satire , and last but not least, shocking subjects used as punchlines. In the '90s, shows like The Simpsons (debuting as early as 1987 as shorts within The Tracey Ullman Show and 1989 as a standalone show) and Beavis and Butt-Head proved to the industry that cartoons with an adult note though in many cases it's more of a teen/young adult audience could be a success, and introduced certain elements, like social satire ( The Simpsons ) and crudeness ( Beavis and Butt-Head ) that paved the way; but the genre really took off with the debut of the Trope Codifier South Park in 1997. Family Guy had a huge impact on the genre as well, particularly in its use of the Cutaway Gag . The massive success of these shows spawned a whole host of imitators and helped initiate a Ruder and Cruder shift within pop culture as a whole. Animated Shock Comedies are virtually always Prime Time Cartoons , and the most common form is the Animated Sitcom . They are character- or gag- driven rather than plot-driven, and often feature Negative Continuity . As part of their "adult" nature, they eschew Zany Cartoon elements like Toon Physics in favor of a more mundane setting where characters typically have jobs and clearly defined species . While many are not afraid to dabble in other genres, like Speculative Fiction or Thriller , this is usually done through parody , and fantastical elements are incorporated in a Mundane Fantastic manner. To maximize the opportunity for offensive jokes, many of the main characters will be total jerks . A major cause behind this trope is the aforementioned Animation Age Ghetto . Historically, any animated media aimed at a general demographic tends to be lumped into the "children's media" bucket: for example, many cartoons in The Golden Age of Animation were aimed at adults no less than children, but were declared "for kids" anyway because once the ghetto had fallen into place, there was little in them that was strictly unsuitable for kids. Even stories built with Multiple Demographic Appeal in mind are taken to mean something an adult can watch with their children without being bored, rather than something an adult should seek out for themselves. Ergo, when creating a series targeted primarily at adults, creators need to convey their intended demographic right from the outset, and the easiest way to do that is to ratchet up the bawdiness. Visually, Animated Shock Comedies are not confined to any particular animation style. The genre's 70s forebearers used a sketchy, Underground Comics -inspired style with much use of cutout-style collage backgrounds; the biggest boom in the late 90s/ early 2000s often used deliberately ugly animation, as a punkish rebuke to "Appeal" in The Twelve Principles of Animation and/or for No Budget reasons; and starting in the The New '10s , less purposefully ugly and more standardized Thin-Line Animation became the most common style. More than other genres, a number of these shows tend to have fairly crude or "cheap" animation styles , possibly to mimic the Genre Popularizer South Park 's " construction paper " style (it's actually animated on computer software and this style allows for very rapid Production Lead Time ). Every popular wave of Animated Shock Comedy received a fair amount of backlash in its respective day, both from Moral Guardians due to the genre's family-unfriendliness and alleged degradation of culture, and from critics for the perceived laziness of its quick gag-based humor. The latter criticism was enhanced by Seasonal Rot and perceived Follow the Leader tendencies amongst some popular examples, many of which lasted well past their expiry date . However, the genre received something of a rehabilitation in the 2010s, with a number of critically-acclaimed examples, like Big Mouth , Rick and Morty and Archer that managed to balance key features of the genre with Character Development and consistent continuity. Remember that Tropes Are Tools and any genre can be done well or poorly. For a long time, Animated Shock Comedies were, for all intents and purposes, the only successful Western animated works aimed at adult audiences. (The stereotype that all adult Western animation fell under this umbrella note to the point that this very page was once called All Adult Animation Is South Park was something of a mirror-image to the idea that All Anime Is Naughty Tentacles .) Part of this is parallel to the inherent issue of the Animation Age Ghetto , being the insistence that "If it's animated, it must be immature". This has begun to change ever so slightly since the late 2010s, with the introduction of the first successful non-comedy adult animated series such as Genndy Tartakovsky's Primal and Castlevania (2017) , but shock comedy is still by far the dominant genre in Western adult animation. Therefore, this is not the place to list aversions. Compare Gross-Out Show , Stoner Flick , Rated M for Money , and All Anime Is Naughty Tentacles .
